name: spec
context: Understand the OpenAPI Specification object model: paths, operations, schemas, components, refs, and security.
info: The OpenAPI Specification defines the structure and semantics of an OpenAPI Description: a set of JSON or YAML documents that describe an HTTP API. Agents use this standard node to reason about valid document shape, root fields, paths, operations, parameters, request bodies, responses, reusable components, JSON Schema dialects, references, security schemes, examples, servers, callbacks, and webhooks. Knowing the spec prevents agents from inventing invalid fields, misplacing schemas, breaking `$ref` resolution, or generating clients from ambiguous operation definitions.
triggers: what fields are valid in openapi
openapi schema object vs json schema
body:
# OpenAPI Specification

An OpenAPI Description is a machine-readable description of an HTTP API. The latest official specification line is OpenAPI 3.2.0. Practical toolchains often use 3.1.0 or 3.0.x, so an agent must know both the current model and the compatibility constraints of its validators, gateways, generators, and documentation tools.

## Root Object

A complete API description normally starts with the OpenAPI Object:

```yaml
openapi: 3.1.0
info:
  title: Example API
  version: 1.0.0
servers:
  - url: https://api.example.com
paths: {}
components: {}
security: []
tags: []
```

At least one meaningful API surface must be described through paths, webhooks, or reusable components. `info` identifies the API, `servers` defines base URLs, `paths` defines callable operations, and `components` stores reusable definitions.

## Paths and Operations

The `paths` object maps URL templates to path items. A path item can contain HTTP method operations such as `get`, `post`, `put`, `patch`, and `delete`. Each operation should have a stable `operationId`, tags, parameters, a request body when applicable, responses, and security overrides.

```yaml
paths:
  /users/{userId}:
    get:
      operationId: getUser
      parameters:
        - name: userId
          in: path
          required: true
          schema:
            type: string
      responses:
        '200':
          description: User found
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/User'
```

## Schemas

OpenAPI 3.1 aligns Schema Objects with JSON Schema 2020-12. Use schemas to define request and response payloads, parameters, headers, and reusable models. Prefer explicit `type`, `required`, `properties`, `items`, enum constraints, numeric bounds, string formats, and examples. Use `$ref` for shared models.

## References

`$ref` points to another schema or object by URI and JSON Pointer. Local references use `#/components/schemas/User`; external references can point to another file or URL. Avoid ambiguous relative references in generated artifacts by bundling with [[openapi/validate]]. Tools must guard against external-resource risk and reference cycles.

## Security

Security schemes live under `components.securitySchemes`. Global `security` applies to all operations unless an operation overrides it. Empty operation-level security means intentionally public. Common schemes are HTTP bearer tokens, API keys, OAuth 2.0, and OpenID Connect.

sections.title: Root object
sections.body: The OpenAPI Object identifies the spec version with `openapi`, describes API metadata with `info`, and exposes callable surfaces through `paths` and optionally `webhooks`. `components` contains reusable definitions. `servers` gives base URLs. `security` defines default authentication requirements. Extension fields must start with `x-`.

sections.title: Paths and operations
sections.body: A path key is a URL template such as `/users/{id}`. Path-level parameters apply to every operation on that path. Operation objects are method-specific definitions with `operationId`, `summary`, `description`, `tags`, `parameters`, `requestBody`, `responses`, `callbacks`, `deprecated`, and `security`. `responses` is required and must document expected status codes or `default`.

sections.title: Schema objects
sections.body: Schemas define JSON shapes and constraints. OpenAPI 3.1 Schema Objects use JSON Schema 2020-12 semantics with OpenAPI additions. Use `type`, `properties`, `required`, `items`, `enum`, `oneOf`, `anyOf`, `allOf`, `format`, `minimum`, `maximum`, `minLength`, and `maxLength` intentionally. For generated clients, favor discriminator-backed `oneOf` over ambiguous unions.

sections.title: Components
sections.body: `components` stores reusable `schemas`, `responses`, `parameters`, `examples`, `requestBodies`, `headers`, `securitySchemes`, `links`, `callbacks`, and `pathItems`. Reuse improves consistency and lets agents change cross-cutting contracts such as error envelopes and auth once.

sections.title: References
sections.body: `$ref` is resolved as a URI plus JSON Pointer. Local refs such as `#/components/schemas/Error` are easiest for tools. External refs are useful for multi-file designs but should be bundled before SDK generation, mocking, or publishing. Avoid cycles unless the target tooling explicitly supports them.

sections.title: Security
sections.body: Define authentication mechanisms in `components.securitySchemes` and apply them with `security` requirements. `type: http` with `scheme: bearer` describes bearer tokens, `type: apiKey` describes header/query/cookie API keys, `type: oauth2` describes OAuth flows, and `type: openIdConnect` points to discovery metadata. Use operation-level `security` to override global defaults.

links.context: Apply the spec model when writing API descriptions
Check whether a document conforms to the spec and local rules
TypeScript schemas that can feed or mirror OpenAPI Schema Objects

related.note: Authoring applies the standard to real API descriptions
Validation catches structural and governance mistakes against the spec
Both are declarative languages that describe a structured interface for tools

frontmatter.author: dojo-community
frontmatter.audience: API designers, backend engineers, AI agents
